speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four. Today is September 15, 1995, interview with Gizel Berman. You were finishing up your story with you were sleeping next to the woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, the fact was that I did her as much I could. And of course, she was a strong woman. And she had her instinct. All she had with her is her nails. And she start to scratch my face. And she did that. And then she gave up and left. And it was quiet for the rest of the night. Never took the shoe. And the next morning I could hardly wait to get up and see who, what will it be like. Well, there we were. She was blue, yellow-- yeah, blue, green, yellow, and purple. And I had noodles hanging through my whole skin, all like half inch noodles hanging down, you know, where she scratched it down. That's what she could do for self defense. We looked at each other. And she was a Polish girl, which means that she must have been in the camp maybe three, four years. So for her, it was a matter of survival in a different way. For me, it was just a brand new learning experience. And we never said a word, walked by each other, and let it go, and never came back again. And the woman had her shoes on for the time we were there. So what I mean, that things like that made me feel good. It wasn't wasted, so something like that. And whenever some older lady, or somebody-- they weren't all older ladies. Anyway, I didn't stay there only for two more days in Auschwitz. And they put us in a train and send us up all the way to no man's lands, to [NON-ENGLISH], which is either Lithuania or-- or southern Prussia, real high up to the next to Scandinavian countries-- Danzig. And what happened there, that too was an extermination camp. But it was also a working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof. And what happened that in no time I fit in all right. I was one of the few who spoke German real, real well and [NON-ENGLISH] Kalway-- that I know his name. It's like you would tell me, I went in my head and took all the unfamiliar-- anyway, and he took to me and used me as an interpreter. And actually, if I-- no, I don't even want to what I remember-- that he tried to call me for some offer or what-- for a doctor. In Germany, they used the doctor's wife gets the title too. Not like in English, you'll never hear it. I was Mrs. Doctor. So he did it maybe not on a regular basis. But he did do it, which was mind-boggling. And I was in charge of the sorting. All the bloody clothes came in from the German front. And the clothes were sorted by-- well, I was in charge so to say. But there were lots of us together. And then it had to go to different places, to the laundry, and fixing, and something,. But it-- it was work. It was work. And we went out from our place to work and came back to sleep in the lager. And we did get to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we get? The morning, it was usually brown water. And if something swim in it, it was just good luck, some-- I don't know-- maybe a little bit of potato or some vegetable. I don't know now. How was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it two or three. No. I don't know. [INAUDIBLE] All I know, we did get a piece of bread and a little piece of margarine with it, and another soup, another watery soup. It was horrible. It was just horrible, horrible. But we did have a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to get more food by other means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did. I did it a few ways. First of all, because I was in the sorting, and we worked outside the camp, and we went by some farmland and so some farmers. And because I could speak Czech or in German-- I don't know what-- the language came in very handy. And I spoke even Russian. Because I had to study Russian too in the school, that I could communicate. And I would steal, let's say, a shawl, or a hat, and something. I would put it underneath-- I don't know how I did it. And since I was pretty full here, I put a lot here, which was stupid because I looked just so much bigger. And it was more kind of suspicious. But no. It worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember once taking four eggs. I-- I was really careful. I was a crazy. Anytime I smuggled something back to camp-- they-- how do you say? They put their hands on us. Searched?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There is a word for it. Not searched. Touching. Doesn't matter. Anyway, they examined us. And whoever was found with something would be shot on the spot. Well, they didn't shoot me. And I did plenty, plenty smuggling. And I smuggled, first of all, because I had all these military things. And I could get a potato. Oh, a potato was a big deal. A potato was a big deal. The egg I just did once. And beets. They gave us like beets, what the cows eat, something like that if I could get a small-- so it was amazing that I did it. And it was a big deal for, let's say, three or four of us who would share it. And some place along the line-- I think maybe it must have been Auschwitz. That's where I was the longest time. I-- yes. My sister-in-law, my brother's oldest-- no. My husband's oldest brother's wife was with us for-- was with me for most of the time. And she started to give me a very bad time, that I'm younger. I should be able to help her. And why don't I do something and help her more. But really, well, I could have so-- doesn't matter. I volunteered to be a night watch in the-- in the lager. And by being a night watch entitled to me a half portion. It was a biggie. It was a biggie thing. From the other hand, every night when an SS came in, first they hit me on my head until I was good and dizzy. They would just hit me. Oh, my head had bumps like you wouldn't believe it, all over just hitting. Well, but the same time I accepted it because that was the price. I couldn't say to them, go out. If they wanted to sleep with somebody, they just came and slept with somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was up to between him and somebody else. If somebody wanted to sleep with a German service-- what are they? I don't know, an SS man, just a soldier, it happened. It did happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, I had to take the beating for it. And I got the bread for it. And my sister-in-law got the half of it. And she was very, very happy. And I was very happy she left me alone. She still didn't survive. She stayed with me all the way. And two months-- maybe two months before liberation she just-- she died. But that was food-wise, the big-- big, big thing. And the work was, as I said, it was horrible. Just bloody clothes, and stinky until they got there I don't know how many weeks later. But I did all kinds of crazy things. I would put a shawl around my mouth or nose, and things. And the fact that this [NON-ENGLISH] was quite human to me-- in fact, he offered me once.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what could I bring you? Would you like something? I says, yes. Soap. I bring you soap. I say, you can not just bring me soap. We all need the soap.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can not bring for everybody soap. I say, thank you very much. Then don't bring me. But I appreciate very much. So then maybe two, three times he came up with such question, what could he do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he really was human to me. And as I said, so that's where I had the nerve to put that little hair into my-- what did I wear? I made myself a turban. Yeah. I made a turban from one of the scarves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't shoot me. He could have easily shoot me. He didn't. And I have that hair sticking out there. And he looked at it. And he didn't say a word. And he let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. The Czech woman who came later, much later in the camp. She arrived to Stutthof. And she was the one. She was an older Czech woman. And there were three of them. And they each had regular hair. And nobody cut it off. They let them stay there with their hair, just those. And she was the one who bring a little silver fork with her. And I bought it. For one of my night watch, I could give her some of the bread. And the same woman, the same woman once-- our kapo's name in Stuffhof was Max. And his thrill was to kick people to death or stamp them to death with his boots. And the minute somebody showed the smallest sign of fear, just kind of shuddering or something that they see him, he killed them right away. So my big thrill was when I walked, let's say some place, from one place to the other, and I saw Max come, I just put a smile on my face from here to there and just walked like that. It was the most horrible, horrible experience each time to encounter him. And what happened, this woman who gave me that hair of hers, one day we were close to our lager. Yes. We were close to our barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just-- she and I were together. And she noticed Max coming behind her towards us too. And she got panicky and pushed me in front of herself kind of on the steps of the barrack, that if by the time-- what did I say? What's his name? Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: Max would get there. He would have to be in contact with me and not with her. So anyway, he got a hold of her and stamped her to death. And he said to me, and you stay here and get rid of the body. And I had to stay there the whole time until she died. And then I had to get some help to drag herself away. These are people, you see, like I knew. I had feelings for each other. And that was Max, the kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the kind of life we had in Stutthof. And every night I was beaten because of being at night watch. And the guys who ever wanted to come in, I had to let them. Anyway, the other thing about Stutthof, well, the cruelty was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, we had to stand four or five hours, sometimes all naked early morning because they want to sort out people for the crematory. And that was a real specialty. Like one officer would go for blue-eyed girls. One would go for skinny arms. The other one would go for thick arms, whatever. You couldn't-- If you would stand on your head, you still couldn't get away with it. Just standing there as straight, I could, you know, and look kind of relaxed, and that's the way they were constantly taking every 20, 30 people for the gas chamber. And not in clothes, but that had to be done because some of them, some let us stand there with their clothes off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can we pass? We're going to go from Stutthof to your death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the death march-- meantime, people were dying like flies. Meantime, people-- well, anybody who just couldn't march every morning had to stand on the side and was left behind. And when the march started two minutes later, one of the soldiers shoot everybody one by one. And otherwise, we had to watch not to step out from the line because that was also destined to be shot. This one morning I couldn't walk. My legs were so swollen. I couldn't stand on my feet. And I said, no matter what my spirit, I can't. And that's all to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So without too much to do, I just stood right away to the end to the side there to be shot. I was standing there. And we were eight. And one girl, who was interesting, whom I didn't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were around 800 marching or something. We got down to, eventually, 200. Doesn't matter. One girl, who stood out was, whom I didn't see before, the same name with mine, Herskovits. I knew her real well. Her father was also a banker of the same bank, only the other place. The name of the bank was a certain, like, Citibank, whatever. So instead of crying, the two of us, we fall on each other and started to laugh and hug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aren't we lucky? We have each other. And we can die now together. Boy, isn't that nice. And we really genuinely meant it, that we thought we were very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happens, like, five minutes later, if that, a little other carriage arrives with one little horse, with one little man sitting in it. It turned out he was the mayor from the next village. And he came there and opened his mouth to the-- to the-- to the SS that he wants to treat the sick ones to a warm meal in the city hall half an hour away with the carriage. And he will take us there. And he should just tell-- he says, no way. I will be shot like always. He says, you can not talk. I am the mayor of this. And they got in a big argument, and really nasty, nasty, nasty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the mayor said, look. You just tell me where you're taking them by the evening. And I'll bring them to you. And you can shoot them tonight. So what's wrong with that? Give them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, all right. So the little mayor took us, put-- We sat on the edge of that little wagon. And well, it was mind-boggling how he got there and how we got to the city hall. And after we got there, it was a room one third of this size. And there was a stove. And around the stove there were about 10 breads on top of the stove. And there was a big pot of warm soup. And he said, everybody get a bread. And everybody gets a bowl of soup. And we thought it's-- it's just unreal. Of course the bread, I took and put it inside my dress underneath my bosom, because I couldn't imagine to eat it myself, that I have to go back to my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the soup. And then he calls out, which one of you speaks German? So I put my hands up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, come. And he took me to a corner room, this little corner room. And he takes out a package of cigarette. And he's trying to offer me a cigarette from the thing. And I thought, I'll faint. And I started to reach out for the cigarette. And I used to smoke. And I just could no more take that than I could have make a I don't know what. My hands were just shaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took out the cigarette, put it in my mouth-- or my hand. He give it to my hand. I put it in my mouth. And he lit up the cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, let me tell you something, young woman. You are here under very, very precarious circumstances. But your present is horrible, I grant you. But there is a-- you have still a future, maybe. I am here in a very comfortable position and everything. And I have a very scary future. And left me, and walked away. And I said to myself, that's funny-- a German mayor says to a Jewish prisoner. I don't know. I must have revived, got back on that carriage. He did take us to the place where we were meeting. And in the night, I took out. And my other girlfriend did the same. We took out the bread and tried to break it in [LAUGHS] I don't know how many little pieces. And gave me enough strength that the next day I could stand on my feet and walk. So but the march was unbelievable. Every place there were hundreds and hundreds of dead bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the march last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were walking, maybe marching about six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you march-- from where to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over there. The Russians are going this way, so we went that way. And if the Russians come that way, then we went this way. Why they didn't shoot us, I could never figure out. And then the last 10 days we were in a barn. And none of us could already-- no. I shouldn't say none of us could walk. If you couldn't go out to pick up your portion, you didn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember I still could drag myself out, and another friend. And we would bring in for the girl. But what happened three days later, we were just lying on top of other dead bodies. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some daughter on top of a dead mother, and some dead mother on top of a dead daughter. It was beyond description. And then one morning the Germans don't come, and don't open the door, and don't yell in, if you want to eat, get out. And it was a Russian who opened up. And he opened the door. And he spit a big one, seeing this pile of dead bodies, or almost really. It was-- 90% was dead. Anyway, and he spit there. He says, you rotten people. If you want something, just come out. We are not putting our food in here in this rotten place. And that was our Russian liberators. And so one very dear friend couldn't move. She had a gangrene leg. And that's all I wanted, to see what will happen to her. So another friend and I, we pulled her out, just like a body, pulled her out on the outside of the barrack, on the outside of the barn. And I did manage to talk to some Russian. And he says, OK. We shall operate right now. And he saw to it. And they took her away wherever to. And then he told us where we can look for her. And one Russian, I ask. He brought me a towel so I could cover my head. And yes. One Russian talked to me like a real human being. And he said, listen, don't eat. You can drop dead just if you overeat now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please-- really, talking like a real human being-- do not. Just the most minimal food, because that's a real killer. So I listened. I did get some food. It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put into a house. Doesn't matter. So they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of your liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated March 11, '45 And then-- and then we were put in a place and kept there for one month. So I don't know how we did survive there. But for one month, the Russian took care of us. And it was cra-- oh, no. No. The first night-- excuse me. The first night of our liberation was still in the barn that they came back. We had to sleep in the barn somehow. First we went out. And then we had to. And then the liberators come in and were raping from one to the other, just like cleaning your shoe or wiping your shoe, or something like that. And I don't know again how I did it. And the first one who came-- and my body was full of boils, millions of boils, but none on my face. But my body was just covered. And looked dark pur-- horrible. Anyway, he came to me. He said, good girl, good-looking girl, fine. We shall make love. I said, officer-- I called him officer. It would be an honor to make love with you. But I don't want you to get my diseases. I have leprosy, syphilis, tuberculosis, and one more-- I don't know what it was. But god, I don't know how they came to my mind, every one of those words. And I said it maybe hundred time that night, the same sentence. I would be honored. Maybe you come back a week later, and I'll be healthy and no more diseases. But I just have syphilis, and tuberculosis, and leprosy. I had more. I had something else. Doesn't matter. I was not raped. I can't tell you how incredible it is. Some of my friends died that night from raping-- 20 times, 30 times, 40 times. And it was the most unbelievable. And these were our liberators. And from then on we had to stay there a whole month to become half-- no, no, no. I just decided a month. After one month, I decided I want out because they still could come in the night. But it wasn't so bad. It wasn't. I don't know why. There was some reason, that maybe some supervisor was there. Something was changed. It was much more human eating wise and sleeping wise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I decided that four of us, we shall go home on our own. And I arranged like a circus group performance. I could speak to Russians. So I was the-- what was I? A spokesperson.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put a bubushka-- it's a towel, I think. And I had my face like this. I could speak very good Russian. I don't speak it to well. Oh, God. And I did it night and day, this face this way. And every time he looked at me, they spit at me or next to me. But they listened. And one girl had to shake all the time. And the third one had to cough. And we were the most horrible looking four monsters. Nobody raped us for three weeks, night and day we were traveling with the Russian soldiers in military cars. If there is miracle, it's-- it's-- it's-- it's-- it's you know when I speak about it I don't want to believe it. I say that about myself. Because it's so unreal how did we-- here and there we would meet somebody. There was no private, no civilians could travel. So it was just with the military. And we made it back to our hometown. We were all from the same hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to come back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was horrible. It looked like a ghost town. They looked at us. Well, we looked like something to look at. And horrible, horrible everything. And so luckily, I had Nick's nurse to whom I went. Everybody had somebody to home to go. I couldn't go to our house. I did go back two days later-- no, next day. The nurse took me. And I found her-- Marishka, the maid we had before, she stayed in the house. And the Russian minister of agriculture moved in and insisted Marishka stays. And the maid's room was all covered from all the way with the pictures of our pictures, the photograph. So those photographs, it's all from Marishka. And she was fantastic. And I found a friend, a partisan, who was a good friend of mind, a man. And he bought me every day 12 french pastries. And Marishka made one dinner. And [INAUDIBLE] made-- I ate full two meals from everything and 24 pastries. And I gained, like, 40 pounds. So when Nick saw me at liberation, and he looked, you look so fat. He was right. I was size 14, really, really, well padded. But that's what I did. And-- and nobody was nice. Nobody was nice, but enough that I had the willpower to wait and see what will happen. And then it happened that Nick sent a letter to his nurse. I am being taken to a hospital. I'm very ill. But I don't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So come up real fast to Prague and go and find me. Come and find me. And there was no way to travel. My luck was with me like miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bumped into a girlfriend who's brother was from our city, little village, Sobrance, who was in the diplomatic corps. And he sent a car for his sister. And so she took me up to Prague in spite of no bridges, no that. I don't know how we did it. We made it up to Prague. I could not find Nick. The people were out for the weekend who I was supposed to gather instruction. And I was besides myself. So I took a cab and went to the YMCA, or YW-- YMCA. And there were hundreds or thousands of people standing, all kinds of little signs, and carrying around, looking for names, and asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there comes a young man up to me. He says, you are Mrs. Dr. Berman? I says, yes. So he said, did you see my sister? I said, no. I said, did you see Dr. Berman? No. So walked away. And I walked there. And I thought him that he's someplace, a hospital. What happened? Three hours later in the same place, a woman is walking on the street. No, no, no, no. In that same place, there was-- and overhears two people saying, guess what? Dr. Berman's wife just arrived. She's right here. She's here with us. But she arrived from home, from Uzhorod. And her husband is here in a hospital. And poor women doesn't know where. And somebody said, well, I just came from a hospital. I was released 10 minutes ago. And a man opened a window and says, I'm Dr. Berman. Did you by any chance meet a woman with the name Gizel Berman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, no. And now he's standing there. They find me. This woman comes and tell me how she talked to Nick that afternoon. By 11 o'clock, I'm in the hospital. They wouldn't let me in. It's pitch dark. And I cry. I scream. I fall on my knees. Eventually, they did let me in. It was pitch dark, except tiny, little lights. I say, you are an idiot. Yet I just walk and look around. And then the very last bed, I see--
